name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا فِى قَرْيَةٍ مِّن نَّبِىٍّ إِلَّآ أَخَذْنَآ أَهْلَهَا بِٱلْبَأْسَآءِ وَٱلضَّرَّآءِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَضَّرَّعُونَ (٩٤)
```

```english
We did not send a prophet to any town without visiting its people with stress and distress so that they might entreat [for Allah’s forgiveness]. (94)
```

```arabic
ثُمَّ بَدَّلْنَا مَكَانَ ٱلسَّيِّئَةِ ٱلْحَسَنَةَ حَتَّىٰ عَفَوا۟ وَّقَالُوا۟ قَدْ مَسَّ ءَابَآءَنَا ٱلضَّرَّآءُ وَٱلسَّرَّآءُ فَأَخَذْنَـٰهُم بَغْتَةً وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ (٩٥)
```

```english
Then We changed the ill [conditions] to good until they multiplied [in numbers] and said, ‘Adversity and ease befell our fathers [too].’ Then We seized them suddenly while they were unaware. (95)
```

```arabic
وَلَوْ أَنَّ أَهْلَ ٱلْقُرَىٰٓ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَٱتَّقَوْا۟ لَفَتَحْنَا عَلَيْهِم بَرَكَـٰتٍ مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَـٰكِن كَذَّبُوا۟ فَأَخَذْنَـٰهُم بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ (٩٦)
```

```english
If the people of the towns had been faithful and Godwary, We would have opened to them blessings from the heaven and the earth. But they denied; so We seized them because of what they used to earn. (96)
```

```arabic
أَفَأَمِنَ أَهْلُ ٱلْقُرَىٰٓ أَن يَأْتِيَهُم بَأْسُنَا بَيَـٰتًا وَهُمْ نَآئِمُونَ (٩٧)
```

```english
Do the people of the towns feel secure from Our punishment overtaking them at night while they are asleep? (97)
```

```arabic
أَوَأَمِنَ أَهْلُ ٱلْقُرَىٰٓ أَن يَأْتِيَهُم بَأْسُنَا ضُحًى وَهُمْ يَلْعَبُونَ (٩٨)
```

```english
Do the people of the towns feel secure from Our punishment overtaking them at midday while they are playing around? (98)
```

```arabic
أَفَأَمِنُوا۟ مَكْرَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ فَلَا يَأْمَنُ مَكْرَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَّا ٱلْقَوْمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ (٩٩)
```

```english
Do they feel secure from Allah’s devising? No one feels secure from Allah’s devising except the people who are losers. (99)
```

```arabic
أَوَلَمْ يَهْدِ لِلَّذِينَ يَرِثُونَ ٱلْأَرْضَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ أَهْلِهَآ أَن لَّوْ نَشَآءُ أَصَبْنَـٰهُم بِذُنُوبِهِمْ ۚ وَنَطْبَعُ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ فَهُمْ لَا يَسْمَعُونَ (١٠٠)
```

```english
Does it not dawn upon those who inherited the earth after its [former] inhabitants that if We wish We will punish them for their sins, and set a seal on their hearts so they would not hear? (100)
```

```arabic
تِلْكَ ٱلْقُرَىٰ نَقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ مِنْ أَنۢبَآئِهَا ۚ وَلَقَدْ جَآءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ فَمَا كَانُوا۟ لِيُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِمَا كَذَّبُوا۟ مِن قَبْلُ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يَطْبَعُ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (١٠١)
```

```english
These are the towns some of whose accounts We recount to you. Their apostles certainly brought them manifest proofs, but they were not the ones to believe in what they had denied earlier. Thus does Allah put a seal on the hearts of the faithless. (101)
```

```arabic
وَمَا وَجَدْنَا لِأَكْثَرِهِم مِّنْ عَهْدٍ ۖ وَإِن وَجَدْنَآ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَفَـٰسِقِينَ (١٠٢)
```

```english
We did not find in most of them any [loyalty to] covenants. Indeed, We found most of them to be transgressors. (102)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses are connected to what preceded them, summarizing the discourse regarding the stories of past nations. They mention that most of them were transgressors (فاسقين) — *those who have stepped outside the bounds of servitude* — failing to fulfill the divine covenant (العهد الإلهي) and the pledge (الميثاق) taken from them on the first day. The verses clarify that this was the reason they fell into the course of specific divine norms (سنن إلهية) that follow one another. This norm is that whenever Allah, the Glorified, sent a prophet from among His prophets to them, He would try and test them with adversity (البأساء) and distress (الضراء). Yet, they would turn away from the signs (آيات) of Allah that called them to return to Him, to supplicate (التضرع), and to turn in repentance (الإنابة) toward Him; they did not take heed through these reminders. This is one norm.

When that proved of no avail, this norm was replaced by another: the sealing (الطبع) of their hearts by hardening them and turning them away from the truth, tethering them to material lusts and the ornaments and vanities of the life of this world. This is the norm of divine maneuvering (المكر) — *the subtle process by which Allah leads the persistent sinner toward their own downfall through seemingly favorable circumstances*.

Then follows a third norm, which is gradual leading (الاستدراج) — *the process of drawing someone closer to destruction by granting them successive blessings despite their disobedience*. This occurs by replacing evil with good, affliction with blessing, and adversity and distress with ease (سراء). In this, they are brought closer day by day and hour by hour toward the divine punishment until it seizes them suddenly while they are unaware. This is because they saw themselves in a cradle of security and peace, rejoicing in the knowledge they possessed and the means at their disposal which they presumed sufficient to repel whatever threatened them with destruction or signaled their demise.

In the course of these verses, Allah, the Glorified, has pointed to a brilliant reality which is the axis upon which the descent of blessings and afflictions upon the human world turns, where He says: **وَلَوْ أَنَّ أَهْلَ الْقُرى آمَنُوا وَاتَّقَوْا لَفَتَحْنا عَلَيْهِمْ بَرَكاتٍ مِنَ السَّماءِ** “If the people of the towns had been faithful and God-conscious, We would have opened to them blessings from the heaven...” (7:96).

The clarification of this is that the world, with all its parts, is interconnected in its components and linked in its extremities. Its parts connect to one another like the connection of the organs and parts of a single body in their health, sickness, and uprightness in performing their functions and fulfilling their duties. Thus, the interaction of effects and properties is ongoing among them, general and all-encompassing.

Everything, as the Noble Quran clarifies, is journeying toward Allah, the Glorified, proceeding toward the goal (الغاية) ordained for it. If the state of some of its parts becomes disordered—especially the noble parts—and its effect weakens and it deviates from its straight path (صراط مستقيم), the effect of its corruption spreads to others. This then reflects back from the other onto itself in the effects that the other sends back to it—effects that are incompatible with the state of this deviated part. This is the trial and calamity (المحنة والبلية) that this cause suffers from the direction of all other causes. If it rights itself, either by itself or with the help of another, its previous state of well-being returns. But if it persists in its deviation and crookedness, and continues its state of corruption, the trial persists for it until, if it transgresses and exceeds its limit and brings the other surrounding causes to the threshold of corruption, all other causes rise against it. They surge with the powers that Allah, the Glorified, has deposited within them to preserve their existences, crushing, leveling, and erasing it suddenly while it is unaware.

This norm, which is among the cosmic norms (السنن الكونية) established by Allah, the Glorified, in the universe, does not fail to apply to man, nor is man an exception to it. Thus, if a nation among nations deviates from the path of the primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — a deviation that blocks it from the human felicity (السعادة) ordained as the goal of its journey in life, this causes a disorder in the state of other surrounding cosmic causes linked to it. Its evil effect reflects back upon it, a result caused only by its deviation from the path and its directing of evil effects from itself toward those causes. At that point, disorders appear in their societies, and general trials in their collective bonds, such as the corruption of morals, hardness of hearts, loss of tender affections, the onslaught of calamities, and the accumulation of misfortunes and cosmic afflictions—such as the sky withholding its rain, the earth its growth, and blessings (البركات) their descent, and the sudden occurrence of floods, deluges, lightning bolts, earthquakes, the sinking of lands, and so on. All these are divine signs (آيات إلهية) that alert man and call the nation to return to its Lord and to go back to what it abandoned of the straight path of the primordial nature (صراط الفطرة المستقيم). It is a trial through hardship after it was tried through ease.

Reflect upon His saying, the Exalted: **ظَهَرَ الْفَسادُ فِي الْبَرِّ وَالْبَحْرِ بِما كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِي النَّاسِ لِيُذِيقَهُمْ بَعْضَ الَّذِي عَمِلُوا لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ** “Corruption has appeared in land and sea because of what the hands of the people have earned, that He may let them taste some of what they have done, so that they may come back” (30:41). You will see it as a speaking witness to this. The verse mentions that the injustices and sins earned by the hands of people necessitate corruption in the land and sea that returns to man—such as the occurrence of wars, the cutting off of roads, the loss of security, and so on—or does not return directly to him, such as the disorder of atmospheric and terrestrial conditions by which man is harmed in his life and livelihood.

Similar to it in a way is His saying, the Exalted: **وَما أَصابَكُمْ مِنْ مُصِيبَةٍ فَبِما كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَيَعْفُوا عَنْ كَثِيرٍ** “Whatever affliction may visit you is because of what your hands have earned, and He excuses many [an offense]” (42:30), according to the exposition of its meaning that will come, Allah willing. Likewise is His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ اللهَ لا يُغَيِّرُ ما بِقَوْمٍ حَتَّى يُغَيِّرُوا ما بِأَنْفُسِهِمْ** “Indeed Allah does not change a people’s lot, unless they change what is in their souls” (13:11), and other verses of similar meaning.

In short, if the nation returns because of that—and how few and rare that is among nations—then so be it. But if they persist in their misguidance and confusion, Allah seals (طبع) their hearts so they become accustomed to it. They begin to think that human life is nothing but this turbulent, wretched life, crowded by the parts of the material world, oppressed by calamities and sorrows, and crushed by the coercion of cosmic nature. They believe that man has nothing but to advance in science and equip himself with intellectual stratagems to combat it, adopting sufficient means to repel its coercion and nullify its maneuvering—just as today means have been adopted sufficient to repel famine, drought, epidemics, plague, and other general contagious diseases, and others to negate floods, deluges, and lightning bolts, and other things brought by the tyranny of nature that threaten the species with destruction.

How man destroys himself; how ungrateful he is! Vanity has seized him, so he thinks that progress in what he calls civilization and science will prepare him to overcome the nature of the universe, nullify its determinations, and coerce it to obey him in his desires and submit to his whims—while he is but one of its parts, governed by its laws, weak in his composition. Had the Truth followed their desires, the heavens and the earth would have been corrupted; and if they were corrupted, weak man would have been among the first of its parts to corrupt and the quickest to perish.

It occurs to him that what religious knowledge aims for is to nullify the attribution of great events to their natural causes (الأسباب الطبيعية), then place their reins in the hand of their Maker so that He becomes one of the partners: other causes having their effects in events—those events whose causes and reasons we can investigate—and the cause which is the Maker having the remaining effects, such as general events and atmospheric occurrences like epidemics, famine, rain, lightning, and others. Then, when he uncovers the natural causes surrounding these matters, he presumes he is in no need of the Lord of the worlds and the governance of His Lordship (الربوبية).

He has missed the fact that Allah, exalted is His Name (عز اسمه), is not a cause in the horizontal plane of causes, nor a reason in the row of material reasons and active forces in nature. Rather, He is the One who encompasses everything, and created every cause, then drove and led it to its effect (المسبب). He gave everything its creation then guided it; none encompasses His creation and His effect but Him. Thus, He may bring about anything through whatever causes He wills—causes unknown to us and absent from our sciences.

To this points the like of His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ اللهَ بالِغُ أَمْرِهِ قَدْ جَعَلَ اللهُ لِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدْراً** “Indeed Allah carries through His command. Certainly Allah has ordained a measure for everything” (65:3). And His saying: **وَاللهُ غالِبٌ عَلى أَمْرِهِ وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يَعْلَمُونَ** “Allah has full command of His affairs, but most people do not know” (12:21). And His saying: **وَما أَنْتُمْ بِمُعْجِزِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَما لَكُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ مِنْ وَلِيٍّ وَلا نَصِيرٍ** “You cannot frustrate [Him] on the earth, and you do not have besides Allah any guardian or helper” (42:31), among other verses.

How can it be possible for man to wage war against Allah in His kingdom, and use his thought to adopt means to nullify His judgment and will? He, the Glorified, is not in the horizontal plane of these means, but in their vertical hierarchy (طولها). That is, He is the One who created man and created from him this will, then the thought, then the adopted means. He placed each in its position and linked some to others from their beginning to their conclusion, until He brought them to the final goal through which man, in his ignorance, wishes to fight his Lord in His decree (القضاء) and destiny (القدر) and contradict Him in His judgment. Yet he is but one of the working hands for what He wills and judges, and one of the causes implementing what He destines and decrees.

To this scandalous human position, the Exalted points after mentioning His seizing of man with adversity and distress, by His saying: **ثُمَّ بَدَّلْنا مَكانَ السَّيِّئَةِ الْحَسَنَةَ حَتَّى عَفَوْا وَقالُوا قَدْ مَسَّ آباءَنَا الضَّرَّاءُ وَالسَّرَّاءُ فَأَخَذْناهُمْ بَغْتَةً وَهُمْ لا يَشْعُرُونَ** “Then We replaced the evil with good until they multiplied and said, ‘Adversity and ease had touched our fathers.’ So We seized them suddenly while they were unaware” (7:95), according to the exposition of the verse’s meaning that will come shortly, Allah the Exalted willing.

This is a demonstrative reality (حقيقة برهانية) establishing that man, like other cosmic species, is linked in his existence to the rest of the parts of the universe surrounding him. His actions in the course of his life and his conduct toward the abode of felicity are linked to others. If they are righteous for the universe, the parts of the universe become righteous for him, and the blessings of heaven are opened for him. If they are corrupt, they corrupt the universe, and the universe meets him with corruption. If he returns to righteousness therein, then so be it; otherwise, he continues in his corruption until, when it takes root in him, the universe rises against him and destroys him by demolishing his structure and erasing his trace, purifying the earth of his filth.

How can it be possible for man, and where would he find the capacity, to oppose the universe with his action when he is but one of its parts that can never be independent of it? Or to outmaneuver it with his thought when he only thinks by arranging universal laws (القوانين الكلية) taken from it? Understand this.

This is a demonstrative reality, and the Noble Quran confirms it and explicitly states it. Allah, the Glorified, is the One who created everything and ordained it with a precise measure, and guided it to what brings it felicity. He did not create the world in vain, nor any of its parts—including man—as a plaything. Rather, He only created what He created so that it might draw near to Him and return to Him. He prepared for man a station of felicity toward which he is driven according to his primordial nature (الفطرة) by the permission of Allah, the Glorified, and He made for him a path that ends at his felicity. If he follows his natural path, then so be it; otherwise, if he deviates from it a deviation that leaves no hope for his return to the evenness of the path, the goal has become void in him, and the word of punishment has become due against him.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَما أَرْسَلْنا فِي قَرْيَةٍ مِنْ نَبِيٍ** (And We did not send to any town a prophet...) to the end of the verse. It is said: **adversity (البأساء)** is in wealth, such as poverty, and **distress (الضراء)** is in the self, such as illness. It is also said: adversity means what reached them of hardship in their selves, and distress what reached them in their wealth. Other things have been said as well. It is said that *ba’s* (البأس) and *ba’sa’* (البأساء) are frequently used for hardship that involves injury and exemplary punishment, as in His saying, the Exalted: **وَاللهُ أَشَدُّ بَأْساً وَأَشَدُّ تَنْكِيلاً** “...and Allah is severer in might and severer in exemplary punishment” (4:84).

Perhaps His saying afterward: **الضَّرَّاءُ وَالسَّرَّاءُ** (distress and ease), where both are intended as what grieves man and what pleases him, serves as a contextual clue (قرينة) for intending the absolute sense of whatever grieves man of hardships by "distress" (الضراء). Thus, His saying: **بِالْبَأْساءِ وَالضَّرَّاءِ** (with adversity and distress) would be the mention of the general after the specific.

The Glorified mentions that the divine norm (السنة الإلهية) has proceeded such that whenever He sent a prophet from among the prophets to a town—and He only sends them to guide them to the path of rectitude—He afflicted them with some hardships in their selves and wealth, hoping that this would move them to supplicate (التضرع) to Him, the Glorified, so that the matter of their call to faith in Allah and righteous deeds (الصالحات) might be completed thereby.

Afflictions and trials are the best aid for the call of the prophets. For as long as man is in blessing, that occupies him from turning toward the One who bestowed it upon him, and he feels self-sufficient through it. But when the blessing is stripped away, he feels the need; humiliation and wretchedness descend upon him, anxiety overcomes him, and annihilation threatens him. This drives him, according to his primordial nature (الفطرة), to seek refuge and supplicate to the One in whose hand is the filling of his void and the removal of his humiliation—and that is Allah, the Glorified, even if he is not consciously aware of Him. When he is alerted to this, his guidance to the truth is to be hoped for. The Exalted said: **وَإِذا أَنْعَمْنا عَلَى الْإِنْسانِ أَعْرَضَ وَنأى بِجانِبِهِ وَإِذا مَسَّهُ الشَّرُّ فَذُو دُعاءٍ عَرِيضٍ** “When We bless man, he is disregardful and turns aside; but when an ill touches him, he is full of prolonged prayer” (41:51).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ثُمَّ بَدَّلْنا مَكانَ السَّيِّئَةِ الْحَسَنَةَ حَتَّى عَفَوْا** (Then We replaced the evil with good until they multiplied...) to the end of the verse. **Replacing (تبديل)** something with something else is to put the second thing in the place of the first. The meanings of "evil" (السيئة) and "good" (الحسنة) are clear; what is meant by them here are things like hardship and ease, fear and security, and distress and ease, as indicated by His saying afterward: **قَدْ مَسَّ آباءَنَا الضَّرَّاءُ وَالسَّرَّاءُ** (Adversity and ease had touched our fathers).

His saying: **حَتَّى عَفَوْا** (until they multiplied) is from *al-afw* (العفو), and it has been interpreted as "multiplicity" (الكثرة); that is, until they increased in wealth and numbers after Allah had diminished them through trials and hardships. It is not far-fetched—though they [the commentators] did not mention it—that it could be from *al-afw* in the sense of "erasing the trace" (إمحاء الأثر), as in the poetic verse:
*A quarter that time has erased (عفاه) through its length, so it vanished,*
*It was nearly wiped away by the length of decay.*
Thus, the meaning would be that they erased, through the "good" (الحسنة) they were given, the traces of the previous "evil" (السيئة), and said: **قَدْ مَسَّ آباءَنَا الضَّرَّاءُ وَالسَّرَّاءُ** (Adversity and ease had touched our fathers). That is, since man is in the world of nature, which is transforming and changing, it is part of the law of his position that distress and ease touch him, and that events—whether they grieve or please him—follow one another without that having any connection to a divine trial or a Lordly punishment.

It is possible, considering this second meaning, that His saying: **وَقالُوا** (and they said...) etc., is an explanatory conjunction (عطف تفسير) for His saying: **عَفَوْا** (they erased). The meaning would be that they erased the mark of the divine trial by their saying: "Distress and ease are but the alternating habits of time; it alternates them for us just as it alternated them for our fathers." As the Exalted said: **وَلئِنْ أَذَقْناهُ رَحْمَةً مِنَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ ضَرَّاءَ مَسَّتْهُ لَيَقُولَنَّ هذا لِي وَما أَظُنُّ السَّاعَةَ قائِمَةً** “And if We let him taste a mercy from Us after some adversity that has touched him, he will surely say, ‘This is my due, and I do not think the Hour will ever set in’” (41:50).

The word **حَتَّى** (until) in His saying: **حَتَّى عَفَوْا وَقالُوا** (until they multiplied and said...) is for the ultimate end (الغاية). The meaning is: Then We gave them blessings in place of punishments, and they became so immersed in them that they forgot the state they were in during the time of hardship and said: "These goods and those evils are from the habit of time." Thus, the sending of hardship then ease ended with them at this goal. It was appropriate for them at that point to remember and be guided to increased gratitude after supplication, but they changed the matter and placed this goal in the place of that goal which their Lord was pleased with for them. Consequently, Allah sealed (طبع) their hearts, so they do not hear the word of truth.

Perhaps in His saying: **الضَّرَّاءُ وَالسَّرَّاءُ** (distress and ease), distress was mentioned before ease to correspond with the order in His saying, the Exalted: **ثُمَّ بَدَّلْنا مَكانَ السَّيِّئَةِ الْحَسَنَةَ** (Then We replaced the evil with good).

In His saying: **فَأَخَذْناهُمْ بَغْتَةً وَهُمْ لا يَشْعُرُونَ** (So We seized them suddenly while they were unaware), there is an allusion to man's ignorance of the course of the divine command. Therefore, the seizing was sudden and unexpected without them perceiving it, while they thought they were knowledgeable about the courses of affairs and the specificities of causes, believing they could avoid the causes of destruction that threatened them through defensive means to which science guides them. The Exalted said: **فَلَمَّا جاءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ فَرِوحُوا بِما عِنْدَهُمْ مِنَ الْعِلْمِ** “When their apostles brought them manifest proofs, they exulted in the knowledge they possessed...” (40:83).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَوْ أَنَّ أَهْلَ الْقُرى آمَنُوا وَاتَّقَوْا لَفَتَحْنا عَلَيْهِمْ بَرَكاتٍ** (If the people of the towns had been faithful and God-conscious, We would have opened to them blessings...) to the end of the verse. **Blessings (البركات)** are types of abundant good that man might be tried by losing, such as security, ease, health, wealth, children, and so on.

His saying: **لَفَتَحْنا عَلَيْهِمْ بَرَكاتٍ مِنَ السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ** (We would have opened to them blessings from the heaven and the earth) contains a metaphor by allusion (استعارة بالكناية). Blessings have been likened to conduits from which flow to them all the favors of Allah they enjoy, but these were blocked from them so that nothing flows to them from them. However, had they believed and been God-conscious, Allah, the Glorified, would have opened them, and there would have flowed to them from them the blessings of the heaven—such as rain, snow, heat, and cold, each in its time and in the beneficial amount—and the blessings of the earth—such as plants, fruits, security, and others. Thus, in the speech, there is a metaphor of "conduits" for "blessings," then some of its requirements and effects were mentioned, which is the "opening" for the thing borrowed for.

In His saying: **وَلَوْ أَنَّ أَهْلَ الْقُرى آمَنُوا وَاتَّقَوْا** (If the people of the towns had been faithful and God-conscious...), there is an indication that the opening of the doors of blessings is caused by the faith and godwariness (تقوى) of all the people of the towns. That is, this is among the effects of the faith and godwariness of the human species, not the faith and godwariness of some. For the faith and godwariness of some is not inseparable from the disbelief and transgression of others, and with that, the cause of corruption is not removed, as is evident.

In His saying: **وَلكِنْ كَذَّبُوا فَأَخَذْناهُمْ بِما كانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ** (But they denied, so We seized them for what they used to earn), there is an indication that the seizing is by way of recompense (المجازاة). It has already been explained in the aforementioned commentary how this occurs, and that in reality, the actions of man are returned to him.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَفَأَمِنَ أَهْلُ الْقُرى أَنْ يَأْتِيَهُمْ بَأْسُنا بَياتاً وَهُمْ نائِمُونَ** (Do the people of the towns feel secure from Our punishment coming to them at night while they are asleep?). **Night-attack (البيات)** and **attacking by night (التبييت)** is to target the enemy at night. It is a form of maneuvering (المكر) because the night is a time of stillness in which man rests and naturally tends to relax and disconnect from others through sleep and quietude.

The content of the verse is branched (فرع) from what preceded it. That is: if this is the state of the people of the towns—that they are deceived by what is under their senses from what lies beyond them, so they are surprised and the punishment seizes them suddenly while they are unaware—then do they feel secure that the punishment of Allah should come to them at night while they are in a state of sleep, having been overcome by heedlessness?

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَوَأَمِنَ أَهْلُ الْقُرى أَنْ يَأْتِيَهُمْ بَأْسُنا ضُحًى وَهُمْ يَلْعَبُونَ** (Do the people of the towns feel secure from Our punishment coming to them in the forenoon while they are playing?). **Forenoon (الضحى)** is the beginning of the day when the sun spreads out. What is meant by **playing (اللعب)** are the actions they occupy themselves with to fulfill the needs of the life of this world and to enjoy the advantages of lusts. These, if they are not in the way of true felicity and seeking the Truth, are "play." Thus, His saying: **وَهُمْ يَلْعَبُونَ** (while they are playing) is an allusion to working for the world. It is sometimes said that it is a metaphor; that is, they occupy themselves with what has no benefit as if they are playing. It is not far-fetched that His saying in the previous verse, **وَهُمْ نائِمُونَ** (while they are asleep), is an allusion to heedlessness. The meaning of the verse is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَفَأَمِنُوا مَكْرَ اللهِ فَلا يَأْمَنُ مَكْرَ اللهِ إِلَّا الْقَوْمُ الْخاسِرُونَ** (Do they feel secure from Allah’s stratagem? No one feels secure from Allah’s stratagem except the people who are losers). He maneuvered (مكر) against him means He touched him with harm or with what ends in harm while he is unaware. This is only valid from Him, the Exalted, if it is by way of recompense (المجازاة)—as when a man commits a sin and Allah takes him with punishment from where he does not perceive, or does to him what leads him to punishment while he does not perceive. As for initiatory maneuvering (المكر الابتدائي) without a prior sin having been realized, it is among the things impossible for Him, the Exalted, as has been repeatedly pointed out.

How subtle is His saying, the Exalted: **أَفَأَمِنَ أَهْلُ الْقُرى** (Do the people of the towns feel secure...) and **أَوَأَمِنَ أَهْلُ الْقُرى** (Or do the people of the towns feel secure...), then His saying: **أَفَأَمِنُوا مَكْرَ اللهِ** (Do they feel secure from Allah’s stratagem?). The third—which is in this verse—is a gathering and summary of the two previous rhetorical denials in the two verses. He made the subject explicit in both verses without saying in the second: "Or do they feel secure..." so that the pronoun in the third verse would return to those in both verses together, as if He took "the people of the towns while they are asleep" as distinct from "the people of the towns while they are playing."

His saying: **فَلا يَأْمَنُ مَكْرَ اللهِ إِلَّا الْقَوْمُ الْخاسِرُونَ** (No one feels secure from Allah’s stratagem except the people who are losers) is because He, the Exalted, clarified in the first two verses that security from the maneuvering of Allah is itself a divine maneuver followed by divine punishment. Thus, those who feel secure from Allah's maneuvering are losers because they are being maneuvered against by this very security.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَوَلَمْ يَهْدِ لِلَّذِينَ يَرِثُونَ الْأَرْضَ مِنْ بَعْدِ أَهْلِها** (Does it not dawn on those who inherit the earth after its [former] inhabitants...) to the end of the verse. It appears that the subject of His saying **يَهْدِ** (dawn/guide) is a pronoun referring to what He summarized of the stories of the people of the towns. His saying **لِلَّذِينَ يَرِثُونَ** (to those who inherit) is its object, made transitive by the letter *lam* because it implies the meaning of "clarification" (التبيين). The meaning is: Has it not become clear—from what We have recited of the stories of the people of the towns—to those who inherit the earth after its inhabitants, as a guide for them? His saying: **أَنْ لَوْ نَشاءُ أَصَبْناهُمْ** (that if We wish We will strike them...) is the object of **يَهْدِ**. What is meant by "those who inherit the earth after its inhabitants" are the successors who inherited the earth from their predecessors.

The sum of the meaning is: Has it not become clear to the successors of those whom We mentioned We seized for their sins—after We tried them then sealed their hearts so they were unable to hear the exhortations of their prophets—that if We wished, We would strike them for their sins without any preventer preventing Us from them or them being able to guard against Our punishment with anything?

It is sometimes said that His saying **يَهْدِ** is treated as an intransitive verb. The meaning is: Has the act of guidance not been performed for them, that if We wished We would strike them for their sins? Similar to it is His saying, the Exalted: **أَوَلَمْ يَهْدِ لَهُمْ كَمْ أَهْلَكْنا مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ مِنَ الْقُرُونِ يَمْشُونَ فِي مَساكِنِهِمْ** “Does it not dawn on them how many generations We have destroyed before them, amid whose dwellings they walk?” (32:26).

As for His saying: **وَنَطْبَعُ عَلى قُلُوبِهِمْ فَهُمْ لا يَسْمَعُونَ** (and We seal their hearts so they do not hear), it is conjoined to His saying **أَصَبْناهُمْ** (We strike them) because the past tense here carries the meaning of the future. The meaning is: Has it not dawned on them that if We wish We will seal... etc. It is also said to be a parenthetical concluding sentence. There are other views and statements regarding the verse that are devoid of benefit.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **تِلْكَ الْقُرى نَقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ مِنْ أَنْبائِها** (Those towns, We relate to you some of their accounts...) to the end of the verse. This is a second summary of their stories related previously, following the summary that passed in His saying: **وَما أَرْسَلْنا فِي قَرْيَةٍ مِنْ نَبِيٍ** (And We did not send to any town a prophet...) to the end of the two or three verses.

The difference between the two summaries is that the first is a summary from the perspective of Allah's action—of His seizing them with adversity and distress, then replacing evil with good, then seizing them suddenly while they were unaware. The second is a summary from the perspective of their own state in the face of the divine call: that although their messengers came to them with manifest proofs, they would not believe because of their prior denial; they were not such as to believe in what they had denied before. This is part of Allah's sealing (الطبع) of their hearts.

His saying: **فَما كانُوا لِيُؤْمِنُوا بِما كَذَّبُوا مِنْ قَبْلُ** (So they were not the ones to believe in what they had denied before). The apparent sense of the verse is that His saying **بِما** (in what) is connected to His saying **لِيُؤْمِنُوا** (to believe). This necessitates that **ما** be a relative pronoun (موصولة). This is supported by His saying, the Exalted, in another place: **فَما كانُوا لِيُؤْمِنُوا بِما كَذَّبُوا بِهِ مِنْ قَبْلُ** “...but they would not believe in what they had denied before” (10:74), for it is more explicit in **ما** being a relative pronoun due to the presence of the pronoun **بِهِ** (in it). The meaning boils down to the fact that they denied what they were called to initially, then they did not believe in it when the prophetic call came a second time.

This is also supported by the apparent form of His saying **فَما كانُوا لِيُؤْمِنُوا** (so they were not the ones to believe), for this construction indicates the negation of a prior predisposition. One says: "I was not one to come to so-and-so" or "I was not one to honor so-and-so when he has done such-and-she," meaning: it was not in my nature to do so, nor was I predisposed to it. In the Revelation: **ما كانَ اللهُ لِيَذَرَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ عَلى ما أَنْتُمْ عَلَيْهِ حَتَّى يَمِيزَ الْخَبِيثَ مِنَ الطَّيِّبِ** “Allah would not leave the faithful in the state you are in, until He has separated the bad from the good” (3:179), meaning: the separation was in His prior will.

And the Exalted said: **لَمْ يَكُنِ اللهُ لِيَغْفِرَ لَهُمْ وَلا لِيَهْدِيَهُمْ سَبِيلاً** “Allah shall not forgive them, nor shall He guide them to any way” (4:137).

It is also supported by His saying in the following verse: **وَما وَجَدْنا لِأَكْثَرِهِمْ مِنْ عَهْدٍ وَإِنْ وَجَدْنا أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَفاسِقِينَ** (We did not find any [loyalty to] covenant in most of them, and We indeed found most of them to be transgressors). The apparent context is that this verse is conjoined as an explanatory conjunction (عطف تفسير) to His saying: **فَما كانُوا لِيُؤْمِنُوا بِما كَذَّبُوا مِنْ قَبْلُ**. Thus, it becomes clear through it that a covenant (عهد) had been made with them, but they transgressed against it and denied it when it was made with them. Then, when the messengers came to them with manifest proofs, they denied them and did not believe in them; they were not such as to believe in what they had denied before.

The verse—that is, His saying: **وَلَقَدْ جاءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ فَما كانُوا لِيُؤْمِنُوا بِما كَذَّبُوا مِنْ قَبْلُ**—is concluded with His saying: **كَذلِكَ يَطْبَعُ اللهُ عَلى قُلُوبِ الْكافِرِينَ** (Thus does Allah seal the hearts of the faithless). This indicates that what He described of the messengers coming with manifest proofs and their lack of faith due to their prior denial is among the instances of the aforementioned sealing (الطبع). Its reality is that Allah fixed the denial in their hearts and empowered it in their souls until, when the messengers came to them with manifest proofs, there was no place for the acceptance of their call because the place was occupied by its opposite.

Thus, these two verses correspond in meaning to the first two verses—that is, His saying: **وَما أَرْسَلْنا فِي قَرْيَةٍ مِنْ نَبِيٍّ إِلَّا أَخَذْنا أَهْلَها** (And We did not send to any town a prophet but that We seized its people...) to the end of the two verses—where they describe the norm of Allah: that He sends signs indicating the truth of the foundations of the call, such as monotheism (التوحيد) and others, by seizing them with adversity and distress, then replacing evil with good, then He seals their hearts as a recompense for their crime.

Accordingly, the meaning in the verse is: Their messengers indeed came to them with manifest proofs, but since they did not believe in the signs sent to them calling them to supplicate to Allah and to be grateful for His favor—but rather doubted them, or even attributed them to the habit of time and the turning of days and the shifting of man from state to state—they denied these signs. This denial became settled in their hearts. So when the prophets called them to the true religion (الدين الحق), they did not believe in the truth they were being called to, nor in the signs they were being reminded of, because they had denied them before. They were not such as to believe in what they had denied before, for Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, had sealed their hearts, so they do not hear.

Thus, their lack of faith is the effect of the divine sealing (الطبع), and the sealing is the effect of their denial of the evidence of the affliction with adversity and distress, then the replacement of evil with good. Evidence for this is His saying: **وَلَقَدْ أَهْلَكْنَا الْقُرُونَ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ لَمَّا ظَلَمُوا وَجاءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ وَما كانُوا لِيُؤْمِنُوا كَذلِكَ نَجْزِي الْقَوْمَ الْمُجْرِمِينَ** “Certainly We destroyed the generations before you when they were unjust, and their apostles brought them manifest proofs, but they would not believe. Thus do We recompense the guilty people” (10:13). And His saying: **ثُمَّ بَعَثْنا مِنْ بَعْدِهِ**—meaning Noah—**رُسُلاً إِلى قَوْمِهِمْ فَجاؤُهُمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ فَما كانُوا لِيُؤْمِنُوا بِما كَذَّبُوا بِهِ مِنْ قَبْلُ كَذلِكَ نَطْبَعُ عَلى قُلُوبِ الْمُعْتَدِينَ** “Then after him We sent apostles to their people. They brought them manifest proofs, but they would not believe in what they had denied before. Thus do We seal the hearts of the transgressors” (10:74). Accordingly, His saying: **فَما كانُوا لِيُؤْمِنُوا بِما كَذَّبُوا مِنْ قَبْلُ** is a branching (تفريع) from His saying: **وَلَقَدْ جاءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ**. What is meant by "what they denied" are the manifest signs that the prophets reminded them of—from the signs of the horizons and the souls and the miraculous signs they brought—for all are His signs. What is meant by "their denial of them before" is their denial of them in terms of the indication of their intellects, upon witnessing them, that they are nurtured by Allah and there is no lord but Him. And by "their lack of faith a second time" is meant their lack of faith in them when the prophets remind them of them.

The meaning is: They were not such as to believe in what the prophets reminded them of and brought of signs—signs which they had denied when their intellects reminded them of them, and which Allah had sent to them so they might remember, supplicate to Him, and be grateful to Him.

Accordingly, what is meant by the "covenant" (العهد) in His saying in the following verse: **وَما وَجَدْنا لِأَكْثَرِهِمْ مِنْ عَهْدٍ وَإِنْ وَجَدْنا أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَفاسِقِينَ** is the covenant that Allah, the Glorified, made with them through the way of the intellect by the tongue of the signs: that they should worship none but Him. What is meant by "transgression" (الفسق) is their stepping out of that covenant by failing to fulfill it.

This covenant has a realization prior to this realization: that Allah, the Glorified, took it specifically from them when He created them and fashioned them through the creation and fashioning of their father Adam, then made him a prototype for general humanity. He made the angels prostrate to him and entered him into Paradise, then made a covenant with him when He commanded his descent to the earth: that he and his progeny should worship Him and not associate anything with Him.

Allah, the Glorified, ordained there what He ordained; thus, according to His ordainment, He guided some and did not guide others. Then, when they entered the world and began their journey in the course of life, the former were guided, while the latter transgressed against His covenant until Allah sealed their hearts and misguidance became due against them in the world after their evil deeds, as previously explained in the commentary on His saying: **كَما بَدَأَكُمْ تَعُودُونَ فَرِيقاً هَدى وَفَرِيقاً حَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ الضَّلالَةُ** “...As He originated you, so shall you return: a part He has guided, and a part has deserved misguidance...” (7:29-30).

The meaning of the verse according to this is: They were not such as to believe at the call of the prophets in what they had denied and not accepted at the taking of the first covenant. We did not find in most of them any fulfillment in the world of the covenant We made there, and We indeed found most of them to be transgressors—stepping out of the judgment of that covenant.

This is one meaning, but it does not contradict the previous meaning, for one of the two meanings is in the vertical hierarchy (طول) of the other and they are not mutually exclusive. For the determination of man's path and his goal of felicity or wretchedness according to the Decree (القدر) does not contradict the possibility of his felicity or wretchedness in the world, nor the contingency of the realization of each upon his choosing and selecting that. There are other views among the people [of tafsir] regarding the interpretation of the verse:

1 — That what is meant by "their denial before" is their denial from the time of the messengers' arrival until the time of persistence and stubbornness; and by His saying: **فَما كانُوا لِيُؤْمِنُوا** etc., their disbelief at the time of persistence. The meaning would be: they were not such as to believe at the time of stubbornness in what they had denied from the beginning of the call until that time. This is a weak view with no evidence from the wording at all.

2 — That what is meant by "their denial before" is their denial of the foundations of the divine laws (أصول الشرائع الإلهية) in which there is no disagreement—such as monotheism (التوحيد), the Return (المعاد), and the issue of the goodness of justice and the ugliness of injustice, for example, which the intellect independently perceives. And by "their denial afterward" is meant their denial of the details of the laws. The meaning would be: they were not such as to believe in these detailed laws, which are what they had denied before in general before the detailed call. This is contrary to the apparent sense of the verse, for disbelief in Allah and other things whose proof is primordial (فطري) to the intellect is not called "denial" (تكذيب). Moreover, the previously mentioned contextual clues contradict it.

3 — That the verse is like His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَوْ رُدُّوا لَعادُوا لِما نُهُوا عَنْهُ** “...and were they sent back, they would surely return to what they were forbidden” (6:28). The meaning would be: they were not such that, if We destroyed them then brought them back to life, they would believe in what they had denied before their destruction. This is the weakest of what has been said in the interpretation of the verse.

4 — That the pronoun in **كَذَّبُوا** (they denied) refers to their predecessors, just as the pronoun in **لِيُؤْمِنُوا** (to believe) is for the successors. The meaning would be: they were not such as to believe in what their predecessors had denied. This is a statement without evidence, and the apparent context of His saying **فَما كانُوا لِيُؤْمِنُوا بِما كَذَّبُوا** is that the referent of all three [pronouns] is one. It is possible to formulate this view in a way that returns to the following view.

5 — That the discourse is based on taking the general people of the towns—from their predecessors and successors—as a single entity to whom the messengers were sent. They are taken as a single person; thus, the denial of the predecessors of their prophets is a denial by the successors, and the lack of faith of the successors is also a lack of faith from the predecessors. This is as the Quran mentions the People of the Book, especially the Jews, then takes their successors to task for what the hands of their predecessors sent forth, attributing the injustices of the former to the latter in many verses. The meaning would be: this is humanity since they were created until today; their messengers came to them with manifest proofs, but the last of them would not believe in what the first of them denied.

However, although this is a correct meaning in itself, the context does not suit it. The speech is directed toward explaining the state of past nations, as indicated by His saying: **تِلْكَ الْقُرى نَقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ مِنْ أَنْبائِها** (Those towns, We relate to you some of their accounts). If they were taken in the sense of a unity extended through the length of their ages—such that it has a beginning and an end, a front and a back, disbelieving in its end and back in what it denied in its beginning and front—it would have been appropriate for the speech to indicate such continuity in His saying: **جاءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ**. It would have said: "their messengers used to come to them with manifest proofs" or what conveys this meaning, not with the like of His saying **جاءَتْهُمْ** (they came to them), which is apparent in considering a single instance or occurrence. Understand this.

This is as in His saying, the Exalted: **كُلَّما جاءَهُمْ رَسُولٌ بِما لا تَهْوى أَنْفُسُهُمْ فَرِيقاً كَذَّبُوا وَفَرِيقاً يَقْتُلُونَ** “...Whenever there came to them an apostle with what their souls did not desire, some they would deny and some they would kill” (5:70). It is known that the deniers were perhaps other than the killers, yet all were attributed to a single society; but it indicated the continuity of the messengers' coming. Similar to it is His saying: **ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُ كانَتْ تَأْتِيهِمْ رُسُلُهُمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ فَقالُوا أَبَشَرٌ يَهْدُونَنا فَكَفَرُوا وَتَوَلَّوْا وَاسْتَغْنَى اللهُ** “That was because their apostles used to bring them manifest proofs, but they said, ‘Shall humans guide us?’ So they disbelieved and turned away, and Allah was self-sufficient...” (64:6). Likewise is His saying in the stories of the prophets after Noah: **ثُمَّ بَعَثْنا مِنْ بَعْدِهِ رُسُلاً إِلى قَوْمِهِمْ فَجاؤُهُمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ فَما كانُوا لِيُؤْمِنُوا بِما كَذَّبُوا بِهِ مِنْ قَبْلُ** “Then after him We sent apostles to their people. They brought them manifest proofs, but they would not believe in what they had denied before” (10:74). The import of His saying "We sent after him apostles to their people" is: We sent each messenger to his people.

6 — That the *ba’* in His saying **بِما كَذَّبُوا** is causal (سببية) and **ما** is an infinitive (مصدرية). What is meant by "their denial before" is what they were accustomed to of denying the messengers or every truth that faced them. The meaning would be: they were not such as to believe in their Lord because of the denial that preceded from them toward the messengers or toward every truth.

This is refuted by the parallel of the verse, which is His saying: **فَما كانُوا لِيُؤْمِنُوا بِما كَذَّبُوا بِهِ مِنْ قَبْلُ**. The presence of the pronoun **بِهِ** (in it) therein is evidence that **ما** is a relative pronoun. Moreover, the apparent sense of the verse is that the *ba’* is for transitivity (تعدية), and **بِما** is connected to His saying **لِيُؤْمِنُوا**. Furthermore, it returns in a way to the first view.

7 — That what is meant by what was pointed to last is their denial which they concealed on the Day of the Covenant (يوم الميثاق). The meaning would be: they were not such as to believe at the call of the prophets in the world in what they had denied before it on the Day of the Covenant.

This is a correct meaning in itself, but it is from the inner (البطن) rather than the outer (الظهر) upon which the interpretation turns. The evidence for this is His saying afterward: **كَذلِكَ يَطْبَعُ اللهُ عَلى قُلُوبِ الْكافِرِينَ** (Thus does Allah seal the hearts of the faithless). It explicitly states that their lack of faith in this manner was only due to the sealing of their hearts, and that Allah sealed their hearts because of their previous denial, so they did not believe in it at the subsequent call. Sealing (الطبع) is not initiatory in the world, but rather for a prior crime within it. This is the best witness that this denial, which bequeathed to them the sealing of their hearts, was in the world; then the sealing necessitated that they not believe in what they had denied before.

Regarding this meaning, there are other verses indicating that the divine sealing (الطبع) and stamping (الختم) are only due to a prior worldly crime. The mere precedence of denial in the Covenant does not result in an initiatory sealing in the world, for that is among the things that do not befit Him, the Glorified, at all. He has said: **يُضِلُّ بِهِ كَثِيراً وَيَهْدِي بِهِ كَثِيراً وَما يُضِلُّ بِهِ إِلَّا الْفاسِقِينَ** “...He misleads many by it, and guides many by it; but He misleads no one by it except the transgressors” (2:26).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَما وَجَدْنا لِأَكْثَرِهِمْ مِنْ عَهْدٍ** (We did not find any [loyalty to] covenant in most of them...) to the end of the verse. It is said in *al-Majma’*: "any covenant" means any fulfillment of a covenant, as it is said: "so-and-so has no covenant," meaning he has no fulfillment of a covenant and is not a preserver of the covenant (end quote). It is permissible that what is meant by the covenant is the covenant of Allah which He made with them through His signs, or their covenant which they made with Allah—that they would worship Him and not associate anything with Him—through the need of their souls and the indication of their intellects. The meaning of the verse has become clear from what preceded.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from al-Husayn ibn al-Hakam, who said: "I wrote to the Righteous Servant (peace be upon him) informing him that I am in doubt—and Abraham said: **رَبِّ أَرِنِي كَيْفَ تُحْيِ الْمَوْتى** (My Lord! Show me how You revive the dead)—so I would like you to show me something of that." He wrote back to him: "Abraham was a believer and loved to increase in faith, but you are a doubter, and there is no good in a doubter." And he wrote: "Doubt only exists as long as certainty (اليقين) has not come; once certainty comes, doubt is not permissible."

And he wrote: "Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: **وَما وَجَدْنا لِأَكْثَرِهِمْ مِنْ عَهْدٍ وَإِنْ وَجَدْنا أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَفاسِقِينَ** (We did not find any [loyalty to] covenant in most of them, and We indeed found most of them to be transgressors)." He said: "It was revealed concerning the doubter."

**I say:** Its application to what passed in the previous commentary is evident. Al-Ayyashi narrated the end of the hadith from al-Husayn ibn al-Hakam al-Wasiti, and in it: "It was revealed concerning the doubters."
